turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 15-680, Bethune-Hill versus The Virginia State Board of Elections.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: After the 2010 Census, the Virginia Legislature redrew the lines for 12 state legislative districts for its House of Delegates. It is undisputed that the state drew these lines with a goal of ensuring that each district would have a black voting-age population of at least 55%. Certain voters challenged these districts as unconstitutional gerrymanders in violation of the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. A three-judge district court, the trial court rejected their claims. As to 11 of the 12 districts, the trial court concluded that the voters had not shown, as this Court's precedents require, that race was the predominant factor motivating the legislature's decision to place a significant number of voters inside or outside the district. The trial court held that race predominates only where there is an actual conflict between the lines drawn and traditional districting criteria and redistricting criteria. The trial court also confined its predominance analysis to those portions of the new line that appeared to deviate from those traditional criteria. The remaining districts, that was for 11 districts, the remaining district was District 75. There, the trial court found that race did predominate, but that the lines as drawn satisfied strict scrutiny. In particular, the court concluded that the legislature had good reasons to believe that the 55% target was necessary in District 75 to avoid diminishing the ability of black voters to elect candidates of their choice. And at that time, at the time of the trial court's decision and at the time of the redistricting, a diminishment would have violated §5 of the Voting Rights Act. As to the first 11 districts, this Court now holds that the trial court employed an incorrect legal standard for racial predominance and it erred in two ways. First, the district court held, the trial court, that in order to show racial predominance, the challengers had to show an actual conflict between the enacted plan and traditional criteria but this Court has previously has held that a party may show predominance either through circumstantial evidence of a district's shape and demographics or through direct evidence of the legislature's purpose. This Court has also previously explained that race may predominate even when a state respects traditional redistricting criteria. The state's theory in this case is irreconcilable with those precedents. Second, the trial court considered the legislature's racial motive only to the extent the challengers identified deviations from traditional criteria that were attributable to race and not some other factor. That approach led the court to give less weight to the 55% racial target and other relevant district-wide evidence. Racial gerrymandering claims however proceed district by district. Courts therefore, must consider all of the district's lines. They should not divorce any portion of the lines whatever their relationship to traditional principles from the rest of the district. Of course, courts may consider evidence pertaining to an area that is larger or smaller than the district at issue. The ultimate object of the inquiry, however, is to determine the legislature's predominant motive for the design of the district as a whole. That kind of holistic analysis is necessary to give the proper weight to racial targets and other kinds of district-wide evidence. Having determined that an incorrect legal standard was applied, this Court in the opinion it issues today, now leaves it to the trial court to apply the correct standard to these 11 districts in the first instance. That leaves us to turn to District 75. As to that district, today's opinion holds that a state's use of race in that legislative district does satisfy strict scrutiny. Because the challengers did not dispute it, this Court assumes without deciding that compliance with §5 is a compelling governmental interest. The question therefore, is whether the state's use of race was narrowly tailored, that is whether the State had good reason to think that a 55% target was necessary to avoid violating §5. The state, this Court now concludes did have good reasons here. The leader of the redistricting effort in the state legislature performed a functional analysis of voting patterns in District 75. He discussed the district with the Incumbent delegate and other members of the house, Black Caucus on numerous occasions. Holding otherwise in this case would afford state legislatures too little breathing room, leaving them trapped between the competing hazards of liability under the Voting Rights Act and the requirements of the Equal Protection Clause. The judgment of the District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia is affirmed in part and vacated in part. Justice Alito has filed a separate opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. Justice Thomas has filed a separate opinion concurring in the judgment in part and dissenting in part.
